transform_insights.name: Loop Fusion
transform_insights.description: Merges two adjacent loops with identical trip counts and compatible dependencies into a single loop. Loop 1 scaled A[i] *= alpha; Loop 2 computed C[i] = A[i] + B[i]. After fusion, both operations happen in one iteration, so A[i] stays in a register between the scale and the add.

why_helped.explanation: Before fusion, loop2 re-loaded A[i] from memory after loop1 wrote it. With large arrays, A had been evicted from L1 between the two loops. After fusion, the scaled A[i] stays in a register — no reload needed.
25% fewer instructions: eliminated one loop's worth of induction variable increment, compare, and branch. Also eliminated the redundant load of A[i] in what was loop2.
42% speedup from combined cache locality improvement and reduced loop overhead.
More useful compute per cycle: the fused body has better instruction-level parallelism (scale and add can overlap in the pipeline) and fewer pipeline flushes from eliminated branches.

trade_offs: Register pressure: The fused body needs registers for A[i], B[i], C[i], alpha, and the intermediate scaled value simultaneously. On register-starved architectures this could cause spills.

Legality: Fusion is only legal when the two loops have identical trip counts and no negative-distance dependencies between them. LLVM checks that loop2's reads of A[i] only depend on loop1's write to the same iteration's A[i], not a different iteration.
Code complexity: The fused loop body is larger, which could inhibit further optimizations like vectorization if the body becomes too complex for the vectorizer's cost model.
